name: Teach the World Foundation
mission: Our mission is to solve the world's illiteracy problem by establishing and deploying effective and scalable models of literacy and learning, through pow...
theoryOfChange: Teach the World Foundation has uploaded a document titled "61951_TTW Theory of Change.pdf" to their profile on the MIT Solve platform, explicitly indicating a published Theory of Change. Additionally, their GlobalGiving profile details "THE TEACH THE WORLD FOUNDATION LEARNING MODEL," which outlines their mission to use digital, game-based learning technologies to address illiteracy, access to education, and teacher shortages in the developing world. This model describes the implementation by facilitators and the rigorous testing of learning technologies to achieve significant learning gains. The organization's website also describes their approach to providing quality education through MicroSchools, digitizing existing traditional classrooms, and implementing smartphone-based learning programs.
scoreSummary: Teach-the-World Foundation Inc shows strong impact and moderate alignment.

score_details.score_summary: Teach-the-World Foundation Inc shows strong impact and moderate alignment.

zakat.claim_evidence: The website for "Teach the World Foundation Pakistan" explicitly states: "Your Donations and Zakat contributions within Pakistan can be made through a variety of donation options, as follows." and provides a dedicated "Zakat Account" for donations within Pakistan. There is no mention of zakat acceptance for the US-based Teach the World Foundation.

dimension_explanations.alignment: The organization is classified as Sadaqah-eligible, offering a medium fit for donors seeking to support educational capacity building within a religious or cultural context [4]. While the cause urgency is high, the alignment score reflects a specialized focus that may not meet all traditional donor requirements for specific giving pathways [5].
dimension_explanations.impact: The foundation demonstrates high impact through evidence-based interventions, showing significant improvements in English alphabet recitation and writing among participants. With a program expense ratio of 78.0% and a Charity Navigator score of 84.0/100, the organization maintains a strong focus on its core mission of literacy [1][5].

Want to support innovative educational models in Pakistan and Bangladesh
Value high transparency and third-party verified outcomes
Prefer lean organizations where every dollar is managed with high efficiency

impact_evidence.evidence_grade_explanation: Grade B reflects verified outcome data from pilot studies showing significant gains in literacy metrics compared to control groups.
impact_evidence.theory_of_change_summary: TTWF uses tablet-based digital learning to enable self-paced literacy acquisition in areas without qualified teachers.
impact_evidence.why_evidence_matters: Evidence ensures that the digital tools provided actually result in functional literacy for children in remote areas.

long_term_outlook.room_for_funding_explanation: With a negative 3-year CAGR and high illiteracy rates in target regions, the foundation has significant capacity to scale its digital model.

peer_comparison.differentiator: Distinguished by its <cite id="2">digital-first microschool model</cite> which reduces reliance on physical infrastructure.

similar_organizations.differentiator: Focuses on K-12 education in North Africa
similar_organizations.name: The Morocco Foundation

strengths.detail: The foundation holds an <cite id="8">overall score of 84.0/100 from Charity Navigator</cite> and a <cite id="9">Gold Seal of Transparency from Candid</cite>.
Third-party evaluations by Nielsen demonstrated that TTWF students reached <cite id="10">91% proficiency in nursery rhyme singing</cite> and <cite id="11">100% mastery of reading direction</cite>.
The organization maintains a <cite id="12">fundraising efficiency of $0.01 per $1 raised</cite>, which is significantly better than industry averages.

strengths_deep_dive: Teach-the-World Foundation distinguishes itself through a <cite id="13">documented theory of change</cite> that prioritizes digital self-learning. Their model is not just theoretical; it is supported by rigorous testing. For example, in English alphabet writing, intervention groups scored <cite id="14">43% compared to only 11%</cite> for control groups. This data-centric approach has earned them recognition as an <cite id="15">Ideas for Action Winner</cite> and a semi-finalist for the Roddenberry Foundation Prize. Furthermore, the organization maintains a <cite id="16">100% independent board of directors</cite>, ensuring high standards of governance and oversight for its international operations.
rich_narrative.summary: Teach-the-World Foundation Inc (TTWF) is a technology-driven non-profit founded in <cite id="1">2016</cite> with a mission to solve the global illiteracy crisis. The organization leverages digital learning models to create scalable educational environments in regions with limited access to traditional schooling. TTWF operates through three primary delivery channels: <cite id="2">Microschools, InSchool programs, and AtHome learning</cite>. By utilizing tablet-based pedagogy, the foundation bypasses the traditional constraints of teacher shortages and infrastructure deficits. The organization currently focuses its efforts on <cite id="3">Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Malawi</cite>, targeting communities where functional literacy remains a significant barrier to economic mobility. Financially, the foundation reported <cite id="4">total revenue of $254,278</cite> in the 2023 tax year. While the organization is lean, it maintains <cite id="5">exceptional fundraising efficiency</cite>, spending only <cite id="6">$0.02 to raise every $1</cite> in contributions. This efficiency ensures that the vast majority of donor support is directed toward the deployment of digital learning tools and the establishment of community-based learning centers. The foundation's model is backed by data-driven outcomes, with pilot studies showing that students in their programs achieved <cite id="7">95% proficiency in English alphabet reading</cite> compared to significantly lower rates in control groups.
